**[NSW](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales")**  **[ACT](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory")**  **[WA](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia")**  **NT**  **[SA](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia")**  **[VIC](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)")**  **[TAS](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania")** [States and territories of Australia](/wiki/File:Northern_Territory_in_Australia.svg "States and territories of Australia")  Location of Northern Territory in Australia Coordinates: [20°S 133°E﻿ / ﻿20°S 133°E﻿ / -20; 133](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Northern_Territory&params=20_S_133_E_) | |

(abbreviated as **NT**; known formally as the **Northern Territory of Australia**[[8]](#cite_note-9) and informally as **the Territory**)[[a]](#cite_note-Territory-1)[[9]](#cite_note-10) is an [Australian internal territory](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") in the central and central-northern regions of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). The Northern Territory shares its borders with [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") to the west ([129th meridian east](/wiki/129th_meridian_east "129th meridian east")), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") to the south ([26th parallel south](/wiki/26th_parallel_south "26th parallel south")), and [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") to the east ([138th meridian east](/wiki/138th_meridian_east "138th meridian east")). To the north, the Northern Territory looks out to the [Timor Sea](/wiki/Timor_Sea "Timor Sea"), the [Arafura Sea](/wiki/Arafura_Sea "Arafura Sea"), and the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria"), including [Western New Guinea](/wiki/Western_New_Guinea "Western New Guinea") and various other islands of the [Indonesian archipelago](/wiki/Indonesian_archipelago "Indonesian archipelago").

The **Northern Territory** (abbreviated as **NT**; known formally as the **Northern Territory of Australia**[[8]](#cite_note-9) and informally as **the Territory**)[[a]](#cite_note-Territory-1)[[9]](#cite_note-10) is an [Australian internal territory](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") in the central and central-northern regions of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). The Northern Territory shares its borders with [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") to the west ([129th meridian east](/wiki/129th_meridian_east "129th meridian east")), [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia") to the south ([26th parallel south](/wiki/26th_parallel_south "26th parallel south")), and [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") to the east ([138th meridian east](/wiki/138th_meridian_east "138th meridian east")). To the north, the Northern Territory looks out to the [Timor Sea](/wiki/Timor_Sea "Timor Sea"), the [Arafura Sea](/wiki/Arafura_Sea "Arafura Sea"), and the [Gulf of Carpentaria](/wiki/Gulf_of_Carpentaria "Gulf of Carpentaria"), including [Western New Guinea](/wiki/Western_New_Guinea "Western New Guinea") and various other islands of the [Indonesian archipelago](/wiki/Indonesian_archipelago "Indonesian archipelago").

The NT covers 1,347,791 square kilometres (520,385 sq mi),[[10]](#cite_note-11) making it the third-largest Australian federal division, and [the 11th-largest country subdivision in the world](/wiki/List_of_country_subdivisions_by_area "List of country subdivisions by area"). It is sparsely populated, with a population of only 260,400[[3]](#cite_note-ABSPop-4) as of March 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit) – fewer than half the population of [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania").[[3]](#cite_note-ABSPop-4) The largest population centre is the capital city of [Darwin](/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory"), having about 52.6% of the Territory's population. The largest inland settlement is [Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs") with a population of about 25,000 people.

The archaeological history of the Northern Territory may have begun more than 60,000 years ago when [humans](/wiki/Human "Human") first settled this region of the [Sahul](/wiki/Sahul_Shelf "Sahul Shelf") Continent. From at least the 18th century, [Makassan](/wiki/Makassar_people "Makassar people") traders began [a relationship with the indigenous people](/wiki/Makassan_contact_with_Australia "Makassan contact with Australia") of the Northern Territory around the trading of [trepang](/wiki/Sea_cucumber_(food) "Sea cucumber (food)"). The coast of the Territory was first seen by Europeans in the 17th century.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The British were the first Europeans to attempt to settle the coastal regions. After three failed attempts to establish a settlement (1824–1828, 1838–1849, and 1864–1866), success was achieved in 1869 with the establishment of a settlement at [Port Darwin](/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory").

The economy is based largely on mining and petroleum, which during 2018–2019 contributed 23% of the [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product"), or $5.68 billion, accounting for 92.4% of exports.[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14)

The Territory's population is concentrated in coastal regions and along the [Stuart Highway](/wiki/Stuart_Highway "Stuart Highway"). Besides the capital of Darwin, the major settlements are (in order of size) [Palmerston](/wiki/Palmerston,_Northern_Territory "Palmerston, Northern Territory"), [Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs"), [Katherine](/wiki/Katherine,_Northern_Territory "Katherine, Northern Territory"), [Nhulunbuy](/wiki/Nhulunbuy "Nhulunbuy") and [Tennant Creek](/wiki/Tennant_Creek "Tennant Creek"). Residents of the Northern Territory are often known as "Territorians".[[14]](#cite_note-15)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Thomas_Baines%2C_Thomas_Baines_with_Aborigines_near_the_mouth_of_the_Victoria_River%2C_N.T%2C_1857.jpg/250px-Thomas_Baines%2C_Thomas_Baines_with_Aborigines_near_the_mouth_of_the_Victoria_River%2C_N.T%2C_1857.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Baines,_Thomas_Baines_with_Aborigines_near_the_mouth_of_the_Victoria_River,_N.T,_1857.jpg)

[Thomas Baines](/wiki/Thomas_Baines "Thomas Baines") with Aboriginal Australians near the mouth of the [Victoria River](/wiki/Victoria_River_(Northern_Territory) "Victoria River (Northern Territory)").

Main article: [History of the Northern Territory](/wiki/History_of_the_Northern_Territory "History of the Northern Territory")

### Pre-colonial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-colonial")]

Further information: [Prehistory of Australia](/wiki/Prehistory_of_Australia "Prehistory of Australia"), [History of Indigenous Australians](/wiki/History_of_Indigenous_Australians "History of Indigenous Australians"), and [Makassan contact with Australia](/wiki/Makassan_contact_with_Australia "Makassan contact with Australia")

Humans have lived in the present area of the Northern Territory since at least 48,400 to 68,700 years ago.[[15]](#cite_note-Clarkson_Jacobs_Marwick_Fullagar_2017_pp._306–310-16)

From the 17th or 18th century AD onwards, traders from [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi") established extensive seasonal trade links with the [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") peoples of [Kimberley region](/wiki/Kimberley_(Western_Australia) "Kimberley (Western Australia)"), the modern-day Northern Territory, and [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land").[[16]](#cite_note-Macknight-2011-17) They collected *trepang* ([sea cucumber](/wiki/Sea_cucumber "Sea cucumber")) for Chinese markets and introduced several goods and technologies to the Indigenous peoples.[[16]](#cite_note-Macknight-2011-17) There is significant evidence of contact with [Makassan](/wiki/Makassar_people "Makassar people") fishers in examples of [Indigenous Australian rock art](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian_rock_art "Indigenous Australian rock art") and [bark painting](/wiki/Bark_painting "Bark painting") of northern Australia, with the Makassan *[perahu](/wiki/Perahu "Perahu")* a prominent feature.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaçonMayFallonTravers2010-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)

### Colonial administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colonial administration")]

With the coming of the British, there were four early attempts to settle the harsh environment of the northern coast, of which three failed in starvation and despair. The land now occupied by the Northern Territory was part of colonial [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") from 1825 to 1863, except for a brief time from February to December 1846, when it was part of the short-lived colony of [North Australia](/wiki/North_Australia "North Australia"). The Northern Territory was part of South Australia from 1863 to 1911, and under its administration the [Overland Telegraph Line](/wiki/Australian_Overland_Telegraph_Line "Australian Overland Telegraph Line") was constructed between 1870 and 1872.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

From its establishment in 1869, the [Port Darwin](/wiki/Port_Darwin "Port Darwin") was the major supply point for the Territory for many decades.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[A railway](/wiki/Palmerston_and_Pine_Creek_Railway "Palmerston and Pine Creek Railway") was built between [Palmerston](/wiki/Palmerston,_Northern_Territory "Palmerston, Northern Territory") and [Pine Creek](/wiki/Pine_Creek,_Northern_Territory "Pine Creek, Northern Territory") between 1883 and 1889 as part of the [North Australia Railway](/wiki/North_Australia_Railway "North Australia Railway"). The economic pattern of cattle raising and mining was established so that by 1911 there were 513,000 cattle. [Victoria River Downs Station](/wiki/Victoria_River_Downs_Station "Victoria River Downs Station"), 686 kilometres (426 mi) west of Darwin,[[20]](#cite_note-21) was at one time the largest [cattle station](/wiki/Cattle_station "Cattle station") in the world.

Gold was found at Grove Hill in 1872 and at [Pine Creek](/wiki/Pine_Creek,_Northern_Territory "Pine Creek, Northern Territory") (in 1871), Brocks Creek, Burundi, and copper was found at [Daly River](/wiki/Daly_River,_Northern_Territory "Daly River, Northern Territory").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

### Federal territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Federal territory")]

On 1 January 1911, a decade after [Federation](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia"), the Northern Territory was separated from South Australia, alongside the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") from NSW, and transferred to federal control. [Alfred Deakin](/wiki/Alfred_Deakin "Alfred Deakin") opined at this time *"[t]o me the question has been not so much commercial as national, first, second, third and last. Either we must accomplish the peopling of the northern territory or submit to its transfer to some other nation."*[[22]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Letters_Patent_Northern_Territory.jpg/250px-Letters_Patent_Northern_Territory.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letters_Patent_Northern_Territory.jpg)

Letters Patent annexing the Northern Territory to South Australia, 1863

In late 1912 there was growing sentiment that the name "Northern Territory" was unsatisfactory.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25) The names "Kingsland" (after King [George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") and to correspond with [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland")), "Centralia" and "Territoria" were proposed with Kingsland becoming the preferred choice in 1913. However, the name change never went ahead.[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

#### Division into North Australia and Central Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Division into North Australia and Central Australia")]

See also: [List of massacres of Indigenous Australians](/wiki/List_of_massacres_of_Indigenous_Australians "List of massacres of Indigenous Australians")

For a brief time between 1927 and 1931 the Northern Territory was divided into [North Australia](/wiki/North_Australia_(territory) "North Australia (territory)") and [Central Australia](/wiki/Central_Australia_(territory) "Central Australia (territory)") at the [20th parallel south](/wiki/20th_parallel_south "20th parallel south"). Soon after this time, parts of the Northern Territory were considered in the [Kimberley Plan](/wiki/Kimberley_Plan "Kimberley Plan") as a possible site for the establishment of a [Jewish Homeland](/wiki/Homeland_for_the_Jewish_people "Homeland for the Jewish people"), understandably considered the "[Unpromised Land](/wiki/Promised_Land "Promised Land")".[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

During the early 20th century, the southern part of the Territory was considered the "last frontier" of Australian settlement, where 'sympathetic whites' hoped that Aboriginal traditions would continue to be practised.[[29]](#cite_note-O'Brien-30) Conflicts arose due to the resource scarcity and the fragility of the cattle industry and the area was rife with Indigenous 'bush bandits' who speared cattle for food for want of employment by ranchers. This was exacerbated by a drought between 1925 and 1929 that led to the deaths of 85 per cent of the children at the [Hermannsburg Mission](/wiki/Hermannsburg,_Northern_Territory "Hermannsburg, Northern Territory") in Central Australia. In the meantime, white attitudes towards Aboriginal people were paternalistic, torn between the desire to help them in times of hunger and the fear of "pauperizing" them and reducing their incentives to work.[[29]](#cite_note-O'Brien-30)

In the 1928 [Coniston massacre](/wiki/Coniston_massacre "Coniston massacre"), punitive expeditions were carried out by white colonists led by [Northern Territory Police](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Police "Northern Territory Police") constable [William George Murray](/wiki/William_George_Murray "William George Murray") in response to the murder of a [dingo](/wiki/Dingo "Dingo") hunter, resulting in the deaths of dozens to hundreds of people of the [Warlpiri](/wiki/Warlpiri_people "Warlpiri people"), [Anmatyerre](/wiki/Anmatyerre "Anmatyerre"), and [Kaytetye](/wiki/Kaytetye_people "Kaytetye people") groups.[[30]](#cite_note-Bradley-31) This was one of many massacres of Aboriginal people in the region.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

#### Post-World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-World War II")]

During World War II, most of the Top End was placed under military government. This is the only time since Federation that part of an Australian state or territory has been under military control. After the war, control for the entire area was handed back to the Commonwealth. The [Bombing of Darwin](/wiki/Bombing_of_Darwin "Bombing of Darwin") occurred on 19 February 1942. It was the largest single attack ever mounted by a foreign power on Australia. Evidence of Darwin's World War II history is found at a variety of preserved sites in and around the city, including ammunition bunkers, airstrips, oil tunnels and museums. The port was damaged in the 1942 Japanese air raids. It was subsequently restored.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

In the late 1960s improved roads in adjoining States linking with the territory, port delays and rapid economic development led to uncertainty in port and regional infrastructure development. As a result of the Commission of Enquiry established by the Administrator,[[33]](#cite_note-34) port working arrangements were changed, berth investment deferred and a port masterplan prepared.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Extension of rail transport was then not considered because of low freight volumes.

[Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") in the territory struggled for rights to land and fair wages. In 1963, the Yolngu people of [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land") submitted two petitions, known as the [Yirrkala bark petitions](/wiki/Yirrkala_bark_petitions "Yirrkala bark petitions"), to the Australian Parliament to protest their land being taken for mining [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite") without prior consultation.[[35]](#cite_note-founding-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) Another important event in this struggle was the [strike and walk off](/wiki/Gurindji_strike "Gurindji strike") by the [Gurindji people](/wiki/Gurindji_people "Gurindji people") at [Wave Hill Cattle Station](/wiki/Kalkaringi "Kalkaringi") in 1966. The [Northern Territory Council for Aboriginal Rights](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory_Council_for_Aboriginal_Rights&action=edit&redlink=1 "Northern Territory Council for Aboriginal Rights (page does not exist)") (NTCAR) supported the strikers and provided publicity.[[37]](#cite_note-ntcar-38) The Federal government of [Gough Whitlam](/wiki/Gough_Whitlam#Prime_Minister "Gough Whitlam") set up [the Woodward Royal Commission](/wiki/Aboriginal_Land_Rights_Commission "Aboriginal Land Rights Commission") in February 1973, to enquire into how land rights might be achieved in the Northern Territory. [Justice Woodward](/wiki/Philip_Woodward_(judge) "Philip Woodward (judge)")'s first report in July 1973 recommended that a [Central Land Council](/wiki/Central_Land_Council "Central Land Council") and a [Northern Land Council](/wiki/Northern_Land_Council "Northern Land Council") be established to present to him the views of Aboriginal people. A Land Rights Bill was drafted, and the *[Aboriginal Land Rights (Northern Territory) Act 1976](/wiki/Aboriginal_Land_Rights_Act "Aboriginal Land Rights Act")* was passed by the [Fraser](/wiki/Malcolm_Fraser "Malcolm Fraser") government on 16 December 1976 and began operation on 26 January 1977).[[38]](#cite_note-39) The Northern Territory Council for Aboriginal Rights was disestablished in 1976.[[37]](#cite_note-ntcar-38)

In 1974, from Christmas Eve to Christmas Day, Darwin was devastated by tropical [Cyclone Tracy](/wiki/Cyclone_Tracy "Cyclone Tracy"). Cyclone Tracy killed 66 people, caused A$837 million in damage (approximately A$8.38 billion as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit)[[39]](#cite_note-40)).[[40]](#cite_note-41) and destroyed more than 70 per cent of Darwin's buildings, including 80 per cent of houses. Tracy left more than 41,000 out of the 47,000 inhabitants of the city homeless. The city was rebuilt with much-improved construction codes and is a modern, landscaped metropolis today.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### Self-government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Self-government")]

In 1978 the Territory was granted self-government by the *[Northern Territory (Self-Government) Act 1978](/wiki/Northern_Territory_(Self-Government)_Act_1978 "Northern Territory (Self-Government) Act 1978")*, with a Legislative Assembly headed by a [Chief Minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_the_Northern_Territory "Chief Minister of the Northern Territory").[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44) The [Administrator of the Northern Territory](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory") is another important position in the government and they act as the King's representative in the Territory and a part of their role is appointing the Chief Minister.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The Territory also publishes official notices in its own *[Government Gazette](/wiki/Northern_Territory_of_Australia_Government_Gazette "Northern Territory of Australia Government Gazette")*.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In the 1980s, conservation-oriented areas in the Northern Territory such as [Kakadu National Park](/wiki/Kakadu_National_Park "Kakadu National Park") and [Uluru-Kata Tjuta National Park](/wiki/Uluru-Kata_Tjuta_National_Park "Uluru-Kata Tjuta National Park") were inscribed on the [UNESCO World Heritage List](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_List "UNESCO World Heritage List") for their mix of natural heritage and Aboriginal culture.

During 1995–96 the Northern Territory was briefly one of the few places in the world with [legal voluntary euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia_in_Australia#Northern_Territory "Euthanasia in Australia"), until the Federal Parliament overturned [the legislation](/wiki/Rights_of_the_Terminally_Ill_Act_1995 "Rights of the Terminally Ill Act 1995").[[46]](#cite_note-47) Before the over-riding legislation was enacted, four people used the law supported by Dr [Philip Nitschke](/wiki/Philip_Nitschke "Philip Nitschke").[[47]](#cite_note-48)[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Northern_Territory_0216.svg/250px-Northern_Territory_0216.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_Territory_0216.svg)

Northern Territory towns, settlements and [road network](/wiki/List_of_highways_in_the_Northern_Territory "List of highways in the Northern Territory").

Main article: [Geography of the Northern Territory](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Northern_Territory "Geography of the Northern Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/ISS036-E-029323_lrg.jpg/250px-ISS036-E-029323_lrg.jpg)](/wiki/File:ISS036-E-029323_lrg.jpg)

The northern coast of Australia is on the left with Melville Island in the lower right[[50]](#cite_note-51)

There are many very small settlements scattered across the Territory, but the larger population centres are located on the single paved road that links Darwin to southern Australia, the [Stuart Highway](/wiki/Stuart_Highway "Stuart Highway"), known to locals simply as "the track".

The Territory is home to many spectacular natural rock formations, including [Uluru / Ayers Rock](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru") and [Kata Tjuta / The Olgas](/wiki/Kata_Tjuta "Kata Tjuta") in the Central Australian region and [Kakadu National Park](/wiki/Kakadu_National_Park "Kakadu National Park") in the north. Each of these sites are sacred to the local Aboriginal people and which have become major International tourist attractions and recognised [UNESCO World Heritage sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)

The northern portion of the Territory is principally [tropical savannas](/wiki/Tropical_and_subtropical_grasslands,_savannas,_and_shrublands "Tropical and subtropical grasslands, savannas, and shrublands"), composed of several distinct [ecoregions](/wiki/List_of_ecoregions_in_Australia "List of ecoregions in Australia") – [Arnhem Land tropical savanna](/wiki/Arnhem_Land_tropical_savanna "Arnhem Land tropical savanna"), [Carpentaria tropical savanna](/wiki/Carpentaria_tropical_savanna "Carpentaria tropical savanna"), [Kimberley tropical savanna](/wiki/Kimberley_tropical_savanna "Kimberley tropical savanna"), [Victoria Plains tropical savanna](/wiki/Victoria_Plains_tropical_savanna "Victoria Plains tropical savanna"), and [Mitchell Grass Downs](/wiki/Mitchell_Grass_Downs "Mitchell Grass Downs"). The southern portion of the Territory is covered in [deserts and xeric shrublands](/wiki/Deserts_and_xeric_shrublands "Deserts and xeric shrublands"), including the [Great Sandy-Tanami desert](/wiki/Great_Sandy-Tanami_desert "Great Sandy-Tanami desert"), [Simpson Desert](/wiki/Simpson_Desert "Simpson Desert"), and [Central Ranges xeric scrub](/wiki/Central_Ranges_xeric_scrub "Central Ranges xeric scrub").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are also an extensive series of river systems in the Territory. These rivers include: the [Alligator Rivers](/wiki/Alligator_Rivers "Alligator Rivers"), [Daly River](/wiki/Daly_River_(Northern_Territory) "Daly River (Northern Territory)"), [Finke River](/wiki/Finke_River "Finke River"), [McArthur River](/wiki/McArthur_River "McArthur River"), [Roper River](/wiki/Roper_River "Roper River"), [Todd River](/wiki/Todd_River "Todd River") and [Victoria River](/wiki/Victoria_River_(Northern_Territory) "Victoria River (Northern Territory)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Hay River is a river south-west of Alice Springs, with the Marshall River, Arthur Creek, Camel Creek and Bore Creek flowing into it.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

The Northern Territory has two distinctive climate zones.

The northern end, including Darwin, has a tropical climate with high humidity and two seasons, the wet (October to April) and dry season (May to September). During the dry season nearly every day is warm and sunny, and afternoon humidity averages around 30%. There is very little rainfall between May and September. In the coolest months of June and July, the daily minimum temperature may dip as low as 14 °C (57 °F), but very rarely lower, and frost has never been recorded.

The [wet season](/wiki/Australian_monsoon "Australian monsoon") is associated with [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclones "Tropical cyclones") and [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") rains. The majority of rainfall occurs between December and March (the southern hemisphere summer), when [thunderstorms](/wiki/Thunderstorms "Thunderstorms") are common and afternoon relative humidity averages over 70% during the wettest months. On average more than 1,570 mm (62 in) of rain falls in the north. Rainfall is highest in north-west coastal areas, where rainfall averages from 1,800 to 2,100 mm (71 to 83 in).

The central region is the [desert](/wiki/Desert "Desert") centre of the country, which includes Alice Springs and [Uluru](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru") (Ayers Rock), and is [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid") with little rain usually falling during the hottest months from October to March. Seasons are more distinct in central Australia, with very hot summers and cool winters. Frost is recorded a few times a year. The region receives less than 250 mm (9.8 in) of rain per year.

The highest temperature recorded in the territory was 48.3 °C (118.9 °F) at [Finke](/wiki/Aputula "Aputula") on 1 and 2 January 1960. The lowest temperature was −7.5 °C (18.5 °F) at Alice Springs on 17 July 1976.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Darwin_%28AU%29%2C_Parliament_House_--_2019_--_4336-8.jpg/250px-Darwin_%28AU%29%2C_Parliament_House_--_2019_--_4336-8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darwin_(AU),_Parliament_House_--_2019_--_4336-8.jpg)

The [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Darwin "Parliament House, Darwin") building in [Darwin](/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory")

### Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parliament")]

Main articles: [Northern Territory Parliament](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Parliament "Northern Territory Parliament"), [Northern Territory Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Northern Territory Legislative Assembly"), and [Proposed Northern Territory statehood](/wiki/Proposed_Northern_Territory_statehood "Proposed Northern Territory statehood")

The Northern Territory Parliament is one of the three [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") parliaments in the country. Based on the [Westminster System](/wiki/Westminster_System "Westminster System"), it consists of the [Northern Territory Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Legislative_Assembly "Northern Territory Legislative Assembly") which was created in 1974, replacing the [Northern Territory Legislative Council](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Legislative_Council "Northern Territory Legislative Council"). It also produces the *[Northern Territory of Australia Government Gazette](/wiki/Northern_Territory_of_Australia_Government_Gazette "Northern Territory of Australia Government Gazette")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Northern Territory Legislative Council was the partly elected governing body from 1947 until its replacement by the fully elected Northern Territory Legislative Assembly in 1974. The total enrolment for the 1947 election was 4,443. The Northern Territory was split into five electorates: Darwin, Alice Springs, Tennant Creek, Batchelor, and Stuart.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

While this assembly exercises powers similar to those of the parliaments of the states of Australia, it does so by legislated [devolution](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") of powers from the [Commonwealth Government](/wiki/Australian_Government "Australian Government"), rather than by any constitutional right. As such, the Commonwealth Government retains the right to legislate for the territory, including the power to override legislation passed by the Legislative Assembly. The [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") is represented by the [Administrator of the Northern Territory](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory"), who performs a role similar to that of a [state governor](/wiki/Governors_of_the_Australian_states "Governors of the Australian states").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Twenty-five members of the Legislative Assembly are elected to four-year terms from single-member [electorates](/wiki/Electoral_district "Electoral district").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Numerous times since self government was granted there has been [agitation for full statehood](/wiki/Proposed_Northern_Territory_statehood "Proposed Northern Territory statehood") within the region.[[57]](#cite_note-58) A [referendum](/wiki/1998_Northern_Territory_referendum "1998 Northern Territory referendum") of voters in the Northern Territory was held on the issue in 1998, which resulted in a 'no' vote.[[58]](#cite_note-59) This was a shock to both the Northern Territory and Commonwealth governments, as opinion polls showed most Territorians supported statehood. But under the Australian Constitution, the federal government may set the terms of entry to full statehood. The Northern Territory was offered three senators, rather than the twelve guaranteed to original states (because of the difference in populations, equal numbers of Senate seats would mean a Territorian's vote for a senator would have been worth more than 30 such votes in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") or [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)")). Alongside what was cited as an arrogant approach adopted by then chief minister [Shane Stone](/wiki/Shane_Stone "Shane Stone"), it is believed that most Territorians, regardless of their general views on statehood, were reluctant to adopt the particular offer that was made.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### Chief minister and cabinet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Chief minister and cabinet")]

Main article: [Northern Territory Government](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Government "Northern Territory Government")

The [chief minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_the_Northern_Territory "Chief Minister of the Northern Territory") is the head of government of a self-governing territory (the head of a state government is a *[premier](/wiki/Premier "Premier")*). The chief minister is appointed by the [administrator](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory"), who in normal circumstances appoints the leader of whichever party holds the majority of seats in the Northern Territory Legislative Assembly. The current chief minister is [Lia Finocchiaro](/wiki/Lia_Finocchiaro "Lia Finocchiaro") of the [Country Liberal Party](/wiki/Country_Liberal_Party "Country Liberal Party"). The CLP defeated Territory Labor to win office on 24 August 2024.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Administrator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Administrator")]

The Northern Territory became self-governing on 1 July 1978 under its own [administrator](/wiki/Administrator_of_the_Northern_Territory "Administrator of the Northern Territory") appointed by the [Governor-General of Australia](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Australia "Governor-General of Australia"). The federal government, not the NT government, advises the governor-general on the appointment of the administrator, but by convention consults first with the Territory government. The current administrator is [David Connolly](/wiki/David_Connolly_(cattleman) "David Connolly (cattleman)") who commenced his term on 27 February 2026.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### Federal government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Federal government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Children_wave_Australian_flags_during_an_Anzac_Day_parade_in_Palmerston%2C_Australia%2C_April_25%2C_2013%2C_as_U.S._Marines_with_the_1st_Platoon%2C_Lima_Company%2C_3rd_Battalion%2C_3rd_Marine_Regiment%2C_Marine_Rotational_130425-M-AL626-014.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Children_wave_Australian_flags_during_an_Anzac_Day_parade_in_Palmerston,_Australia,_April_25,_2013,_as_U.S._Marines_with_the_1st_Platoon,_Lima_Company,_3rd_Battalion,_3rd_Marine_Regiment,_Marine_Rotational_130425-M-AL626-014.jpg)

Children wave Australian flags during an [Anzac Day](/wiki/Anzac_Day "Anzac Day") parade in Palmerston

The Northern Territory is represented in the [federal parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia") by two members in the House of Representatives and two members in the Senate. As of May 2022, resulting from the [2022 federal election](/wiki/2022_Australian_federal_election "2022 Australian federal election"), [Marion Scrymgour](/wiki/Marion_Scrymgour "Marion Scrymgour") from the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") (ALP) in [Lingiari](/wiki/Division_of_Lingiari "Division of Lingiari") and [Luke Gosling](/wiki/Luke_Gosling "Luke Gosling") from the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") (ALP) in [Solomon](/wiki/Division_of_Solomon "Division of Solomon") serve in the House of Representatives, and [Malarndirri McCarthy](/wiki/Malarndirri_McCarthy "Malarndirri McCarthy") from the ALP and [Jacinta Nampijinpa Price](/wiki/Jacinta_Nampijinpa_Price "Jacinta Nampijinpa Price") from the [Country Liberal Party](/wiki/Country_Liberal_Party "Country Liberal Party") serve in the Senate.

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Local government")]

The Northern Territory is divided into [seventeen local government areas](/wiki/Local_government_areas_of_the_Northern_Territory "Local government areas of the Northern Territory"): two cities, three municipalities, nine regions, and three shires. Shire, city and town councils are responsible for functions delegated by the Northern Territory parliament, such as road infrastructure and waste management. Council revenue comes mostly from property taxes and government grants.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### Aboriginal land councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Aboriginal land councils")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Australia_Aboriginal_Culture_011.jpg/250px-Australia_Aboriginal_Culture_011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australia_Aboriginal_Culture_011.jpg)

Aboriginal Australians own about 49% of the Northern Territory's land

Further information: [Aboriginal land councils in the Northern Territory](/wiki/Aboriginal_land_councils_in_the_Northern_Territory "Aboriginal land councils in the Northern Territory")

[Aboriginal land councils](/wiki/Aboriginal_land_council "Aboriginal land council") in the Northern Territory are groups of Aboriginal landowners, set up under the *[Aboriginal Land Rights Act 1976](/wiki/Aboriginal_Land_Rights_Act_1976 "Aboriginal Land Rights Act 1976")*.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### Political parties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Political parties")]

The two historically dominant political parties in the Northern Territory are the conservative [Country Liberal Party](/wiki/Country_Liberal_Party "Country Liberal Party") which governed the Territory from 1974 to 2001, from 2012 to 2016 and since 2024, and the social-democratic [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_(Northern_Territory_Branch) "Australian Labor Party (Northern Territory Branch)") which governed the Territory from 2001 to 2012 and from 2016 to 2024. Minor parties that are also active in the NT include the [Northern Territory Greens](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Greens "Northern Territory Greens"), the [Shooters and Fishers Party](/wiki/Shooters_and_Fishers_Party "Shooters and Fishers Party") and various others. It is common for [independent politicians](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") to win elections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The CLP's rule was once so tight, that a former minister once said the CLP had a "'rightful inheritance of being the party that runs this place'".[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)

The [2024 Northern Territory general election](/wiki/2024_Northern_Territory_general_election "2024 Northern Territory general election") saw the [NT Greens](/wiki/NT_Greens "NT Greens") win a seat in the Legislative Assembly, with [Kat McNamara](/wiki/Kat_McNamara "Kat McNamara") defeating former Chief Minister [Natasha Fyles](/wiki/Natasha_Fyles "Natasha Fyles") in the seat of [Nightcliff](/wiki/Electoral_division_of_Nightcliff "Electoral division of Nightcliff"); this marked the first time the Greens entered the Legislative Assembly in the history of the Northern Territory.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

As fewer parties and candidates contest Northern Territory general elections than they do Australian federal elections in the Northern Territory, the CLP, Labor and independents usually have a higher vote share at territory elections than at federal elections in the Northern Territory due to the absence of right-wing minor parties such as [Pauline Hanson's One Nation](/wiki/Pauline_Hanson%27s_One_Nation "Pauline Hanson's One Nation") and the fact that the Greens do not run in every seat at territory elections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The population of the Northern Territory at the 2011 Australian census was 211,945,[[70]](#cite_note-2011census-71) a 10 per cent increase from the 2006 census. The [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics") estimated a June 2015 resident population of 244,300, taking into account residents overseas or interstate. The territory's population represents 1% of the total population of Australia.[[71]](#cite_note-abs-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)[[73]](#cite_note-ads-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Darwin_homebanner.jpg/250px-Darwin_homebanner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darwin_homebanner.jpg)

[Darwin](/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory") is the capital and largest city in the Northern Territory

The Northern Territory's population is the youngest in Australia and has the largest proportion (23.2%) under 15 years of age and the smallest proportion (5.7%) aged 65 and over. The [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of residents of the Northern Territory is 31 years, six years younger than the national median age.[[70]](#cite_note-2011census-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Alice_Springs_%282049606825%29.jpg/250px-Alice_Springs_%282049606825%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alice_Springs_(2049606825).jpg)

[Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs")

Indigenous Australians make up 30.3% of population[[74]](#cite_note-75) and own some 49% of the land. The life expectancy of Aboriginal Australians is well below that of non-Indigenous Australians in the Northern Territory, a fact that is mirrored elsewhere in Australia. ABS statistics suggest that Indigenous Australians die about 11 years earlier than the average non-Indigenous Australian. There are Aboriginal communities in many parts of the territory, the largest ones being the [Pitjantjatjara](/wiki/Pitjantjatjara_people "Pitjantjatjara people") near [Uluru / Ayers Rock](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru"), the [Arrernte](/wiki/Arrernte_people "Arrernte people") near Alice Springs, the [Luritja](/wiki/Luritja "Luritja") between those two, the [Warlpiri](/wiki/Warlpiri_people "Warlpiri people") further north, and the [Yolngu](/wiki/Yolngu "Yolngu") in eastern [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land").

In 2019, 147,255[[75]](#cite_note-76) people lived in Darwin, an overwhelming majority of the Territory's population. Despite this, the Northern Territory is the least urbanised jurisdiction in the Commonwealth (followed by [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Australians "Chinese Australians") (3.1%)
* [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Australians "Indian Australians") (2.3%)
* [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Australians "Italian Australians") (2.1%)
* [Greek](/wiki/Greek_Australian "Greek Australian") (2%)
* [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Australians "Dutch Australians") (1.3%)

31.2% of the population was born overseas at the 2016 census. The five largest groups of overseas-born were from the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") (2.6%), [England](/wiki/England "England") (2.4%), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") (2%), [India](/wiki/India "India") (1.6%) and [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") (0.6%).[[77]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-83)[[78]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-84)

25.5% of the population, or 58,248 people, identified as [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") ([Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders")) in 2016.[[N 10]](#cite_note-90)[[77]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-83)[[78]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-84)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Languages")]

At the 2021 census, 57.3% of the population spoke only [English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English") at home. The other languages most commonly spoken at home were [Kriol](/wiki/Australian_Kriol "Australian Kriol") (2.2%), [Djambarrpuyngu](/wiki/Dhuwal_language "Dhuwal language") (1.7%), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") (1.4%) and [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") (1.3%).[[80]](#cite_note-census2021-91)[[77]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-83)[[78]](#cite_note-censusdata.abs.gov.au-84)

There are more than 100 [Aboriginal languages](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_languages "Australian Aboriginal languages") and dialects spoken in the Northern Territory,[[81]](#cite_note-92) in addition to English which is most common in cities such as Darwin or Alice Springs. Major indigenous languages spoken in the Northern Territory include [Murrinh-patha](/wiki/Murrinh-patha_language "Murrinh-patha language") and Ngangikurrungurr in the northwest around [Wadeye](/wiki/Wadeye "Wadeye"), [Warlpiri](/wiki/Warlpiri_language "Warlpiri language") and [Warumungu](/wiki/Warumungu_language "Warumungu language") in the centre around [Tennant Creek](/wiki/Tennant_Creek "Tennant Creek"), [Arrernte](/wiki/Upper_Arrernte_language "Upper Arrernte language") around [Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs"), [Pintupi-Luritja](/wiki/Pintupi_dialect "Pintupi dialect") to the south east, [Pitjantjatjara](/wiki/Pitjantjatjara_language "Pitjantjatjara language") in the south near [Uluru / Ayers Rock](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru"), [Yolngu Matha](/wiki/Yolngu_Matha "Yolngu Matha") to the far north in [Arnhem Land](/wiki/Arnhem_Land "Arnhem Land") (where the dialect Djambarrpuyngu of [Dhuwal](/wiki/Dhuwal_language "Dhuwal language") is considered a lingua franca), and [Burarra](/wiki/Burarra_language "Burarra language"), [Maung](/wiki/Maung_language "Maung language"), [Iwaidja](/wiki/Iwaidja_language "Iwaidja language") and [Kunwinjku](/wiki/Bininj_Kunwok_language "Bininj Kunwok language") in the centre north and on [Croker Island](/wiki/Croker_Island "Croker Island") and the [Goulburn Islands](/wiki/Goulburn_Islands "Goulburn Islands"). [Tiwi](/wiki/Tiwi_language "Tiwi language") is spoken on [Melville Island](/wiki/Melville_Island_(Australia) "Melville Island (Australia)") and [Bathurst Island](/wiki/Bathurst_Island_(Northern_Territory) "Bathurst Island (Northern Territory)").[[82]](#cite_note-93) Literature in many of these languages is available in the [Living Archive of Aboriginal Languages](/wiki/Living_Archive_of_Aboriginal_Languages "Living Archive of Aboriginal Languages").

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religious Affiliation (2021)[[80]](#cite_note-census2021-91)

1. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") (40.5%)
2. [No Religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Australia "Irreligion in Australia") (38.5%)
3. Hinduism (2.70%)
4. Buddhism (2.40%)

In the 2021 census, Christianity is the major Religious affiliation in Northern Territory followed by 40.5% of its population. In 1971, Christianity was followed by 70.2% of the population and it has been declining since, while percentage of people who identified as having no religious affiliation has increased from 17.9% in 1971 to 38.5% in 2021.[[80]](#cite_note-census2021-91)

[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") is the territory's largest non-Christian religion (2.7%), followed by [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (2.1%) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (1.4%).[[80]](#cite_note-census2021-91)[[83]](#cite_note-94)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Charles_Darwin_University%2C_NT.jpg/250px-Charles_Darwin_University%2C_NT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Darwin_University,_NT.jpg)

[Charles Darwin University](/wiki/Charles_Darwin_University "Charles Darwin University")

### Primary and secondary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Primary and secondary")]

A Northern Territory school education consists of six years of primary schooling, including one transition year, three years of middle schooling, and three years of secondary schooling. In the beginning of 2007, the Northern Territory introduced Middle School for Years 7–9 and High School for Years 10–12, however as of 2026, the territory is transitioning middle schools into high schools in order to align with the rest of the country. Northern Territory children generally begin school at age five. On completing secondary school, students earn the [Northern Territory Certificate of Education and Training](/wiki/Northern_Territory_Certificate_of_Education_and_Training "Northern Territory Certificate of Education and Training") (NTCET). Students who successfully complete their secondary education also receive a tertiary entrance ranking, or ATAR score, to determine university admittance.

Northern Territory schools are either publicly or privately funded. Public schools, also known as state or government schools, are funded and run directly by the Department of Education.[[84]](#cite_note-95) Private fee-paying schools include schools run by the Catholic Church and independent schools, some elite ones similar to English [public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(UK) "Public school (UK)"). Some Northern Territory Independent schools are affiliated with [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox "Greek Orthodox") or [Seventh-day Adventist Churches](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"), but include non-church schools and an Indigenous school.

As of 2009, the Northern Territory had 151 public schools, 15 Catholic schools and 21 independent schools. 39,492 students were enrolled in schools around the territory with 29,175 in public schools, and 9,882 in independent schools. The Northern Territory has about 4,000 full-time teachers.

### Tertiary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Tertiary")]

The Northern Territory has [one university](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Australia "List of universities in Australia") which opened in 1989 under the name of the [Northern Territory University](/wiki/Northern_Territory_University "Northern Territory University").[[85]](#cite_note-96) Now renamed as the [Charles Darwin University](/wiki/Charles_Darwin_University "Charles Darwin University"), it had about 19,000 students enrolled: about 5,500 higher education students and about 13,500 students on vocational education and training (VET) courses. The first tertiary institution in the territory was the [Batchelor Institute of Indigenous Tertiary Education](/wiki/Batchelor_Institute_of_Indigenous_Tertiary_Education "Batchelor Institute of Indigenous Tertiary Education") which was established in the mid-1960s.

### Libraries and archives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Libraries and archives")]

[Library & Archives NT](/wiki/Library_%26_Archives_NT "Library & Archives NT") is the Northern Territory's public research library and archival organisation and it supports the development of library services across the Territory by supporting public libraries.[[86]](#cite_note-97)[[87]](#cite_note-98) Library & Archives NT was formed in 2019 following the merge of the Northern Territory Archives Service and the Northern Territory Library.[[88]](#cite_note-99)

The [National Archives of Australia](/wiki/National_Archives_of_Australia "National Archives of Australia") also have a repository in Darwin, this is collocated with the Library & Archives NT repository at the Northern Territory Archives Centre.[[89]](#cite_note-100)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Australian economy](/wiki/Australian_economy "Australian economy")

### Mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Mining")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Ranger_Uranium_Mine_in_Kakadu_National_Park.jpeg/250px-Ranger_Uranium_Mine_in_Kakadu_National_Park.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ranger_Uranium_Mine_in_Kakadu_National_Park.jpeg)

[Ranger Uranium Mine](/wiki/Ranger_Uranium_Mine "Ranger Uranium Mine") in the [Kakadu National Park](/wiki/Kakadu_National_Park "Kakadu National Park")

The Northern Territory's economy is largely driven by [mining](/wiki/Mining_in_the_Northern_Territory "Mining in the Northern Territory"), which is concentrated on energy producing [minerals](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral"), petroleum and energy and contributes around $2.5 billion to the [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_regional_domestic_product "Gross regional domestic product") and employs over 4,600 people. Mining accounts for 14.9% of the gross state product in 2014–15 compared to just 7% nationally.[[90]](#cite_note-101)

In recent years, largely due to the effect of major infrastructure projects and mine expansions, construction has overtaken mining as the largest single industry in the territory. Construction, mining and manufacturing, and government and community services, combine to account for about half of the territory's gross state product (GSP), compared to about a third of national gross domestic product (GDP).[[91]](#cite_note-Economy-Overview-102)

The economy has grown considerably over the past decade, from a value of $15 billion in 2004–05 to over $22 billion in 2014–15. In 2012–13 the territory economy expanded by 5.6%, over twice the level of national growth, and in 2014–15 it grew by 10.5%, four times the national growth rate.[[91]](#cite_note-Economy-Overview-102)

Between 2003 and 2006 the gross state product had risen from $8.67 billion to $11.476 billion, an increase of 32.4%. During the three years to 2006–2007 the Northern Territory gross state product grew by an average annual rate of 5.5%. Gross state product per capita in the Northern Territory ($72,496) is higher than any Australian state or territory and is also higher than the gross domestic product per capita for Australia ($54,606).

The Northern Territory's exports were up 12.9% or $681 million in 2012–13. The largest contributor to the territory's exports was: mineral fuels (largely LNG), crude materials (mainly mineral ores) and food and live animals (primarily live cattle). The main international markets for territory exports are Japan, China, Indonesia, the United States and Korea.[[92]](#cite_note-103)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Imports to the Northern Territory totalled $2,887.8 million which consisted of mainly machinery and equipment manufacturing (58.4%) and petroleum, coal, chemical and associated product manufacturing (17.0%).[[93]](#cite_note-ABS_Economics-104)

The principal mining operations are bauxite at Gove Peninsula where the production is estimated to increase 52.1% to $254 million in 2007–08, manganese at Groote Eylandt, production is estimated to increase 10.5% to $1.1 billion which will be helped by the newly developed mines include Bootu Creek and Frances Creek, gold which is estimated to increase 21.7 per cent to $672 million at the Union Reefs plant and uranium at Ranger Uranium Mine.[[94]](#cite_note-105)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Tourism is an important economic driver for the territory and a significant industry in regional areas.[[95]](#cite_note-economicprofile.com.au-106) Iconic destinations such as [Uluru / Ayers Rock](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru") and [Kakadu](/wiki/Kakadu_National_Park "Kakadu National Park") make the Northern Territory a popular destination for domestic and international travellers. Diverse [landscapes](/wiki/Landscapes "Landscapes"), [waterfalls](/wiki/Waterfalls "Waterfalls"), wide open spaces, [aboriginal culture](/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_culture "Australian Aboriginal culture") and wild and untamed [wildlife](/wiki/Wildlife "Wildlife") provides the opportunity for visitors to immerse themselves in the natural wonder that the Northern Territory offers. In 2015, the territory received a total of about 1.6 million domestic and international visitors contributing an estimated $2.0 billion to the local economy. Holiday visitors made up the majority of total visitation (about 792,000 visitors).

Tourism has strong links to other sectors in the economy including accommodation and food services, retail trade, recreation and culture, and transport.[[95]](#cite_note-economicprofile.com.au-106)

[Wide Open Space](/wiki/Wide_Open_Space_(festival) "Wide Open Space (festival)") is an annual festival of music, arts and culture[[96]](#cite_note-hist-107) that takes place over three days at the [Ross River Resort](/w/index.php?title=Ross_River_Resort&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ross River Resort (page does not exist)") in the [McDonnell Ranges](/wiki/McDonnell_Ranges "McDonnell Ranges"),[[97]](#cite_note-events-108) around 80 km (50 mi) east of Alice Springs.[[98]](#cite_note-109) over three days in April/May.[[97]](#cite_note-events-108)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Transport")]

See also: [Transportation in Australia](/wiki/Transportation_in_Australia "Transportation in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/LasseterHighway.JPG/250px-LasseterHighway.JPG)](/wiki/File:LasseterHighway.JPG)

The [Lasseter Highway](/wiki/Lasseter_Highway "Lasseter Highway") connects [Uluru](/wiki/Uluru "Uluru") (Ayers Rock) to the [Stuart Highway](/wiki/Stuart_Highway "Stuart Highway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/NR45_%2B_NR10_%2B_Ghan_Alice_Springs%2C_2015_%2802%29.JPG/250px-NR45_%2B_NR10_%2B_Ghan_Alice_Springs%2C_2015_%2802%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:NR45_%2B_NR10_%2B_Ghan_Alice_Springs,_2015_(02).JPG)

[The Ghan](/wiki/The_Ghan "The Ghan")

The Northern Territory is the most sparsely populated state or territory in Australia.

The NT has a connected network of sealed roads, including two national highways, linking with adjoining states and connecting the major territory population centres, and other important centres such as Uluru (Ayers Rock), [Kakadu](/wiki/Kakadu_Highway "Kakadu Highway") and Litchfield National Parks. The Stuart Highway, once known as "The Track", runs north to south, connecting Darwin and [Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs") to [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"). Some of the sealed roads are single-lane bitumen. Many unsealed (dirt) roads connect the more remote settlements.

The fatigue resulting from long-distance driving and the hazards inherent in dirt roads, wildlife, water crossings and wild weather have led the Northern Territory Government to pursue road safety campaigns in English and several Aboriginal languages. Persuading people to drive at the right speed for the road conditions has been a key goal.[[100]](#cite_note-111)[[101]](#cite_note-112) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit), the Northern Territory's road vehicle speed limit in built-up areas was 60 kilometres per hour unless the town had gazetted a lower default speed limit: many had chosen 50 km/h or lower. Outside most built-up areas the default speed limit was 110 km/h unless a speed limit sign stated otherwise. Reflecting the nature of the topography and very low population density, some sections of the Arnhem, Barkly, Stuart and Victoria highways had a maximum speed of 130 km/h.[[102]](#cite_note-113)

In 2004, a [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") railway was opened between Alice Springs and Darwin, completing the [Adelaide–Darwin rail corridor](/wiki/Adelaide%E2%80%93Darwin_rail_corridor "Adelaide–Darwin rail corridor") and bringing to fruition the dream of a transcontinental railway initiated in 1878 with the [Central Australia Railway](/wiki/Central_Australia_Railway "Central Australia Railway") from the south and the [North Australia Railway](/wiki/North_Australia_Railway "North Australia Railway") from the north – but with a gap of more than 800 km (500 mi) between Alice Springs and [Birdum](/wiki/Birdum,_Northern_Territory "Birdum, Northern Territory") still to be bridged when the antique 1067 mm (3 ft 6 in) narrow-gauge railway was closed in 1976.[[103]](#cite_note-Fuller-114) The line carries fast freight trains and one passenger train: [The Ghan](/wiki/The_Ghan "The Ghan") experiential tourism train that runs between Darwin and Adelaide, stopping in the NT at [Katherine](/wiki/Katherine,_Northern_Territory "Katherine, Northern Territory"), [Tennant Creek](/wiki/Tennant_Creek "Tennant Creek"), [Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs") and [Kulgera](/wiki/Kulgera,_Northern_Territory "Kulgera, Northern Territory").

[Darwin International Airport](/wiki/Darwin_International_Airport "Darwin International Airport") is the major domestic and international airport for the territory. Several smaller airports are also scattered throughout the territory and are served by smaller airlines, including [Alice Springs Airport](/wiki/Alice_Springs_Airport "Alice Springs Airport"), [Ayers Rock Airport](/wiki/Ayers_Rock_Airport "Ayers Rock Airport"), [Katherine Airport](/wiki/RAAF_Base_Tindal "RAAF Base Tindal") and [Tennant Creek Airport](/wiki/Tennant_Creek_Airport "Tennant Creek Airport").

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Media")]

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Print")]

The Northern Territory has only one daily tabloid newspaper, [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation_(1980%E2%80%932013) "News Corporation (1980–2013)")'s *[Northern Territory News](/wiki/Northern_Territory_News "Northern Territory News"),* or *NT News*. *[The Sunday Territorian](/wiki/Northern_Territory_News "Northern Territory News")* is the sister paper to the *NT News* and is the only dedicated Sunday tabloid newspaper in the Northern Territory.

The *[Centralian Advocate](/wiki/Centralian_Advocate "Centralian Advocate")* is circulated around the [Alice Springs](/wiki/Alice_Springs "Alice Springs") region twice a week. There are also five weekly community newspapers. The territory receives the national daily, *[The Australian](/wiki/The_Australian "The Australian")*, while *[The Sydney Morning Herald](/wiki/The_Sydney_Morning_Herald "The Sydney Morning Herald"), [The Age](/wiki/The_Age "The Age")* and the [*Guardian Weekly*](/wiki/The_Guardian_Weekly "The Guardian Weekly") are also available in Darwin. Katherine's paper is the *[Katherine Times](/w/index.php?title=Katherine_Times&action=edit&redlink=1 "Katherine Times (page does not exist)")*.

There is an LGBT community publication, QNews Magazine,[[104]](#cite_note-115) which is published in Darwin and Alice Springs.

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Television")]

Metropolitan Darwin has had five broadcast television stations:

* [ABC](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") Northern Territory – (**[ABD](/wiki/ABD_(TV_station) "ABD (TV station)")**)
* [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service") Northern Territory – (**[SBS](/wiki/SBS_Television "SBS Television")**)
* [Southern Cross Seven](/wiki/Seven_(Southern_Cross_Austereo) "Seven (Southern Cross Austereo)") – (**[TND](/wiki/TND_(TV_station) "TND (TV station)")**) – *[Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") Affiliate*
* [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") Darwin – (**[NTD](/wiki/NTD_(Australian_TV_station) "NTD (Australian TV station)")**)
* [Ten Darwin](/wiki/DTD_(TV_station) "DTD (TV station)") (or Darwin Digital Television) – (**[DTD](/wiki/DTD_(TV_station) "DTD (TV station)")**) – *[Network Ten](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10") Affiliate*

Darwin also has four open-narrowcast stations:

* [First Nations TV](/wiki/First_Nations_Broadcasting_Australia "First Nations Broadcasting Australia")
* [Darwin TV](/wiki/First_Nations_Broadcasting_Australia "First Nations Broadcasting Australia")
* [First Nations Tourism TV](/wiki/First_Nations_Broadcasting_Australia "First Nations Broadcasting Australia")
* [Business TV](/wiki/First_Nations_Broadcasting_Australia "First Nations Broadcasting Australia")

Regional Northern Territory has a similar availability of stations:

* [ABC](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") Northern Territory – (**[ABD](/wiki/ABD_(TV_station) "ABD (TV station)")**)
* [SBS](/wiki/Special_Broadcasting_Service "Special Broadcasting Service") Northern Territory – (**[SBS](/wiki/SBS_Television "SBS Television")**)
* [Seven Central](/wiki/Seven_Central "Seven Central") – (**[QQQ](/wiki/QQQ "QQQ")**) – *[Seven Network](/wiki/Seven_Network "Seven Network") Affiliate*
* [Imparja](/wiki/Imparja_Television "Imparja Television") – (**[IMP](/wiki/Imparja "Imparja")**) – *[Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network "Nine Network") Affiliate*
* [Ten Central](/wiki/Central_Digital_Television "Central Digital Television") (or Central Digital Television) – (**[CDT](/wiki/Central_Digital_Television "Central Digital Television")**) – *[Network Ten](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10") Affiliate*

Remote areas are generally required to receive television via the [Viewer Access Satellite Television](/wiki/Viewer_Access_Satellite_Television "Viewer Access Satellite Television") service, which carries the same channels as the regional areas, as well as some extra open-narrowcast services, including [Indigenous Community Television](/wiki/Indigenous_Community_Television "Indigenous Community Television") and [Westlink](/wiki/Westlink_(Australian_TV_channel) "Westlink (Australian TV channel)").

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Radio")]

Darwin has radio stations on both AM and FM frequencies. ABC stations include [ABC Radio Darwin](/wiki/ABC_Radio_Darwin "ABC Radio Darwin") (105.7FM), [ABC Radio National](/wiki/Radio_National "Radio National") (657AM), [ABC News Radio](/wiki/ABC_NewsRadio "ABC NewsRadio") (102.5FM), [ABC Classic FM](/wiki/ABC_Classic "ABC Classic") (107.3FM) and [Triple J](/wiki/Triple_J "Triple J") (103.3FM). The two commercial stations are [Hot 100 FM](/wiki/Hot_100_(Darwin_radio_station) "Hot 100 (Darwin radio station)") (8HOT) and [Mix 104.9](/wiki/Mix_104.9 "Mix 104.9") (8MIX). The leading community stations are [104.1 Territory FM](/wiki/104.1_Territory_FM "104.1 Territory FM") (8TOP) and [94.5 Radio Larrakia](/wiki/First_Nations_Radio "First Nations Radio") (8KNB).

The radio stations in Alice Springs are also broadcast on the AM and FM frequencies. ABC stations include Triple J (94.9FM), ABC Classic FM (97.9FM), [783 ABC Alice Springs](/wiki/ABC_Alice_Springs "ABC Alice Springs") (783AM), ABC News Radio (104.1FM) and ABC Radio National (99.7FM). There are two community stations in the town—[CAAMA](/wiki/Central_Australian_Aboriginal_Media_Association "Central Australian Aboriginal Media Association") (100.5FM) and 8CCC (102.1FM). The commercial stations, which are both owned by the same company are [Sun 96.9](/wiki/Sun_969 "Sun 969") (96.9FM) and [8HA](/wiki/8HA "8HA") (900AM). Christian radio station [Vision Christian Radio](/wiki/Vision_Christian_Radio "Vision Christian Radio") (88.0FM). Two additional stations, Territory FM (98.7FM) and [Radio TAB](/wiki/RadioTAB "RadioTAB") (95.9FM) are syndicated from Darwin and Brisbane, respectively.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Territory&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-77)** According to the [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics"), Significant Urban Areas are defined to represent significant towns and cities, or agglomerations of smaller towns, that have at least 10,000 total population. Significant Urban Areas may contain more than one distinct Urban Centre. There are urban areas of greater than 10,000 people that the ABS does not currently classify as Significant Urban Areas.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** The ABS currently only defines two Significant Urban Areas within the Northern Territory.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-79)** According to the [Australian Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics"), Urban Centres represent areas of concentrated urban development with populations of 1,000 people or more.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-80)** Only the 10 most populus Urban Centres are shown. For a complete list see: [List of places in the Northern Territory by population](/wiki/List_of_places_in_the_Northern_Territory_by_population "List of places in the Northern Territory by population")
5. **[^](#cite_ref-81)** Only the 10 most populus Local Government Areas are shown. For a complete list see: [List of places in the Northern Territory by population](/wiki/List_of_places_in_the_Northern_Territory_by_population "List of places in the Northern Territory by population")
6. **[^](#cite_ref-85)** In accordance with the Australian Bureau of Statistics source, [England](/wiki/England "England"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") and the Special Administrative Regions of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") are listed separately
7. **[^](#cite_ref-86)** As a percentage of 199,426 persons who nominated their ancestry at the 2016 census.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-87)** Of any ancestry. Includes those identifying as [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") or [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders"). Indigenous identification is separate to the ancestry question on the Australian Census and persons identifying as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander may identify any ancestry.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-89)** The Australian Bureau of Statistics has stated that most who nominate "Australian" as their ancestry are part of the [Anglo-Celtic](/wiki/Anglo-Celtic_Australian "Anglo-Celtic Australian") group.[[79]](#cite_note-88)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-90)** Of any ancestry. Includes those identifying as [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") or [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders"). Indigenous identification is separate to the ancestry question on the Australian Census and persons identifying as Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander may identify any ancestry.

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Territory_1-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Territory_1-1) While the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") is also an Australian internal territory and indeed more populous than the Northern Territory, it is informally referred to as the "ACT", or simply “[Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra")”. While the demonym for a resident of the Northern Territory is simply "Territorian", the demonym for a resident of the Australian Capital Territory is "Canberran".
